content.body:
Pressure = Force / Area
P = F/A  (Pa = N/m²)

Fluid pressure:
P = ρgh  (ρ=density, g=9.8 m/s², h=depth)
Pressure same at same depth (Pascal's law)
Pressure acts in all directions in a fluid

Atmospheric pressure:
~101,325 Pa = 1 atm = 101.3 kPa
Measured by barometer (760 mmHg)

content.title: Pressure Calculations
content.body:
Elephant foot: mass=5000kg, foot area=0.04m²
Weight=50000N, 4 feet
P=50000/(4×0.04)=312,500 Pa

High heels: mass=60kg, heel area=0.0001m²
P=600/0.0001=6,000,000 Pa → dents floor!

Water pressure at 10m depth:
P=1000×9.8×10=98,000 Pa (≈1 atm extra)

content.title: Pascal's Law
content.body:
Hydraulic systems: pressure transmitted through fluid
Hydraulic press: F₁/A₁ = F₂/A₂
Small force on small piston → large force on large piston
Car brakes, hydraulic jack, digger

questions.question: Pressure formula
questions.explanation: Pressure=Force÷Area. SI unit: Pascal (Pa)=N/m²

questions.question: Units of pressure
questions.explanation: Pascal (Pa) = Newton per square metre (N/m²)

questions.question: Force 500N on area 0.01m². Pressure=
Water pressure at depth h: P=
questions.explanation: P=ρgh: density×gravity×depth

questions.question: Pressure at 5m depth in water (ρ=1000, g=10)
Hydraulic press A₁=2cm², A₂=100cm², F₁=10N. F₂=
Why do snowshoes work?
options: Distribute weight over large area → reduce pressure on snow
questions.answer: Distribute weight over large area → reduce pressure on snow
questions.explanation: Same force spread over larger area → less pressure per unit area → don't sink into snow

questions.question: Atmospheric pressure is measured by
questions.explanation: Barometer: height of mercury column supported by atmospheric pressure. Standard: 760mmHg=101.3kPa

questions.question: Pressure depends on depth, NOT on
questions.explanation: P=ρgh depends only on depth (h), density and g. Not on container shape or horizontal dimensions

questions.question: Pascal's Law states
options: pressure transmitted equally in all directions through enclosed fluid
questions.answer: pressure transmitted equally in all directions through enclosed fluid
questions.explanation: Pascal's Law: pressure applied to enclosed fluid transmitted unchanged to all parts → basis of hydraulics

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body:
Boyle's Law (constant temperature):
P₁V₁ = P₂V₂
Pressure and volume inversely proportional

Atmospheric pressure decreases with altitude
(less atmosphere above)

Manometer: measures gas pressure difference
Simple manometer: U-tube with liquid

Boyle's Law molecular explanation:
Fewer molecules per volume → fewer collisions → lower pressure

content.title: Boyle's Law Problems
content.body:
Syringe at 1 atm, 200mL. Compressed to 50mL:
P₂=P₁V₁/V₂=1×200/50=4 atm

Gas at 150kPa, 0.5L expands to 1.5L:
P₂=150×0.5/1.5=50 kPa

Diver at 10m depth (P≈2atm, 0.5L air):
At surface: V=2×0.5/1=1L

content.title: Everyday Examples
content.body:
Suction cup: removing air reduces pressure inside
Atmosphere holds water in inverted tube (barometer)
Lungs: diaphragm increases chest volume → pressure drops → air flows in
Cycling pump: compress air → pressure increases

questions.question: Boyle's Law states (constant T)
questions.explanation: Boyle's: P₁V₁=P₂V₂. Pressure and volume inversely proportional at constant temperature

questions.question: Gas at 2 atm, 3L compressed to 1L at constant T. New pressure=
questions.explanation: P₂=P₁V₁/V₂=2×3/1=6 atm

questions.question: Atmospheric pressure decreases with altitude because
options: fewer air molecules above → less weight pressing down
questions.answer: fewer air molecules above → less weight pressing down
questions.explanation: Pressure = weight of air column above. Higher altitude → less air above → less pressure

questions.question: Breathing in (inhaling) works because
options: diaphragm contracts expanding chest volume → pressure inside falls below atmosphere → air flows in
questions.answer: diaphragm contracts expanding chest volume → pressure inside falls below atmosphere → air flows in
questions.explanation: Boyle's law in breathing: increased volume → decreased pressure below atmospheric → pressure gradient drives air inflow

questions.question: Scuba diver's lungs hold 0.5L at 20m depth (P=3atm). At surface volume =
questions.explanation: Boyle's: V₂=P₁V₁/P₂=3×0.5/1=1.5L (must exhale while ascending!)

questions.question: Suction cup sticks to wall because
options: air pressure outside pushes it against wall as low pressure created inside
questions.answer: air pressure outside pushes it against wall as low pressure created inside
questions.explanation: Pressing suction cup removes air inside → outside atmospheric pressure pushes cup against surface

questions.question: Gas at 500kPa, 2L expands at constant T to 5L. Pressure =
Manometer measures
options: pressure difference between gas and atmosphere
questions.answer: pressure difference between gas and atmosphere
questions.explanation: Manometer: U-tube with liquid. Height difference shows pressure difference between two sides

questions.question: P₁V₁=P₂V₂ graph of P vs V is
questions.explanation: PV=const is equation of hyperbola: P=k/V. As V increases, P decreases along curve

questions.question: Tyre pressure in cold weather decreases because
options: gas molecules slow down → fewer/softer collisions per unit area → lower pressure (approx Boyle's + Charles's)
questions.answer: gas molecules slow down → fewer/softer collisions per unit area → lower pressure (approx Boyle's + Charles's)
questions.explanation: Lower temperature: less kinetic energy → less forceful collisions → lower pressure (if volume fixed, from combined gas law)

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body:
Surface tension:
Force per unit length acting at liquid surface
γ = F/L  (N/m)
Due to unbalanced cohesive forces at surface
Meniscus: curved liquid surface at container wall
  Water: concave (wets glass — adhesion>cohesion)
  Mercury: convex (doesn't wet glass — cohesion>adhesion)

Viscosity:
Resistance to flow
Higher viscosity = thicker fluid (honey, oil)
Viscosity decreases with temperature (liquids)

content.title: Surface Tension Examples
content.body:
Water strider: insect walks on water surface
Raindrop: spherical (minimise surface area)
Soap bubble: surface tension of two surfaces
Capillary action: liquid rises in narrow tube
  h = 2γcosθ/(ρgr)
Wetting agent (detergent): reduces surface tension

content.title: Stokes' Law
content.body:
Terminal velocity of sphere in viscous fluid:
F_drag = 6πηrv (η = dynamic viscosity)
At terminal velocity: weight = drag + upthrust

Measuring viscosity: drop steel ball through oil,
measure terminal velocity, use Stokes' law

questions.question: Surface tension is due to
options: unbalanced cohesive forces at liquid surface
questions.answer: unbalanced cohesive forces at liquid surface
questions.explanation: Surface molecules: cohesive forces from sides but not above → net inward force → surface behaves like elastic skin

questions.question: Water meniscus in glass tube is concave because
questions.explanation: Water wets glass: adhesion to glass > cohesion to itself → surface curves up at edges → concave meniscus

questions.question: Mercury meniscus in glass is convex because
options: cohesion (Hg-Hg) > adhesion (Hg-glass) — mercury doesn't wet glass
questions.answer: cohesion (Hg-Hg) > adhesion (Hg-glass) — mercury doesn't wet glass
questions.explanation: Mercury doesn't wet glass: stronger attraction to itself than to glass → surface curves down at edges → convex meniscus

questions.question: Detergent reduces water surface tension allowing it to
options: wet fabrics and remove grease more effectively
questions.answer: wet fabrics and remove grease more effectively
questions.explanation: Surfactant (detergent) molecules reduce surface tension: water spreads better, penetrates fabric, emulsifies grease

questions.question: Viscosity of a liquid when heated
questions.explanation: Heating liquid: more kinetic energy disrupts molecular bonds/friction → easier flow → lower viscosity

questions.question: Stokes' law: drag on sphere =
questions.explanation: Stokes' Law: F=6πηrv for slow flow. η=viscosity, r=radius, v=velocity

questions.question: Capillary action works because
options: adhesion between liquid and tube walls pulls liquid up against gravity
questions.answer: adhesion between liquid and tube walls pulls liquid up against gravity
questions.explanation: Capillary rise: adhesion causes liquid to wet tube wall → surface tension pulls liquid up along wall → rises until weight balances

questions.question: A steel needle floats on water due to
options: surface tension supporting it (surface deforms without breaking)
questions.answer: surface tension supporting it (surface deforms without breaking)
questions.explanation: Surface tension acts along bent water surface upward → supports weight of needle despite steel density > water

questions.question: Spherical raindrop minimises
questions.explanation: Sphere has minimum surface area for given volume → minimises surface energy (proportional to area) → stable shape

questions.question: Blood viscosity increase (e.g. dehydration) affects health because
options: heart works harder to pump thicker blood → cardiovascular strain
questions.answer: heart works harder to pump thicker blood → cardiovascular strain
questions.explanation: Higher viscosity blood: more resistance in vessels → higher blood pressure → increased cardiac workload → risk of clotting

questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Pressure Fluids concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of physics.
Core Theory

This section of Pressure Fluids builds on foundational knowledge. In physics, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 4.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Pressure Fluids. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 4.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Pressure Fluids. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Pressure Fluids include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Pressure Fluids. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

questions.question: Which quantity is a vector?
questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector quantity — it has both magnitude and direction. Speed is a scalar (magnitude only).

questions.question: What is the principle of conservation of energy?
options: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
Energy is always lost as heat
questions.answer: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of energy states that the total energy in a closed system remains constant.

questions.question: What is the unit of power?
questions.explanation: Power is measured in Watts (W). 1 W = 1 J/s. Power is the rate of energy transfer.

questions.question: What is the boiling point of water at standard pressure?
questions.explanation: Water boils at 100°C (373 K) at standard atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa).

questions.question: In a series circuit, the current:
options: Is the same through all components
Is largest at the first component
Is zero if one component fails
questions.answer: Is the same through all components
questions.explanation: In a series circuit, there is only one path for current, so the same current flows through all components.

questions.question: What is pressure?
questions.explanation: Pressure P = F/A, where F is force applied perpendicular to a surface area A. SI unit is Pascal (Pa).

questions.question: Which type of radiation has the highest penetrating power?
questions.explanation: Gamma rays are electromagnetic radiation with very short wavelength and highest penetrating power, requiring thick lead or concrete to stop.

questions.question: What happens to light when it travels from air into glass?
questions.explanation: Light slows down when entering a denser medium (glass) and changes direction — this is refraction.

questions.question: State the law of conservation of momentum.
options: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.answer: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.explanation: In a closed system with no external forces, the total momentum before a collision equals the total momentum after.

questions.question: What is the half-life of a radioactive substance?
options: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
The time for all nuclei to decay
The time to double in activity
questions.answer: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
questions.explanation: Half-life is the time taken for the activity (or number of radioactive nuclei) to decrease to half its original value.
